title: Introduction to GPU Computing
description: Understand why parallel computing matters, how GPU architecture differs from CPUs, and when to use heterogeneous computing. Covers Moore's Law, the power wall, SIMT execution, and Amdahl's Law.

sections.title: The End of the Free Lunch
sections.content:
For decades, software developers enjoyed a remarkable free ride. Every 18 months or so, CPUs got faster -- your same code ran quicker without changing a line. This phenomenon was driven by **Moore's Law**, the observation by Intel co-founder Gordon Moore in 1965 that the number of transistors on a chip doubles roughly every two years.

But here is the crucial distinction most people miss: **Moore's Law is about transistor count, not clock speed.** For a long time, more transistors meant higher clock frequencies. CPUs went from 5 MHz (Intel 8086, 1978) to 200 MHz (Pentium Pro, 1995) to 3.8 GHz (Pentium 4, 2004). Then something hit a wall.

### The Power Wall

The relationship between clock frequency and power consumption is roughly:

```
Power ~ Voltage^2 * Frequency
```

As frequency increases, voltage must also increase to maintain signal integrity. Since voltage scales roughly linearly with frequency at the limit, total power scales as roughly **frequency cubed** (P proportional to f^3). The result is that **power consumption grows cubically** with clock speed. By 2004, the Pentium 4 Prescott was consuming 115 watts and running at temperatures that required increasingly absurd cooling solutions. Intel was projecting that their next-generation chips would need nuclear-reactor-level cooling.

This is the **power wall**: we cannot keep increasing clock speed because chips would melt. The timeline below illustrates the trend:

<!-- TODO: SVG -->
```
  Clock Speed Over Time: The Power Wall
  
  Freq (GHz)
  4.0 |                           X Pentium 4 (115W!!)
      |                          /
  3.0 |                        /
      |                      /
  2.0 |               Core 2 Duo (65W) -- clock DROPS,
      |              /  O                  cores DOUBLE
  1.0 |           /
      |        /
  0.5 |     X Pentium III (25W)
      |   /
  0.0 X---+-------+-------+-------+-------+------->
     1993  1996   1999   2002   2006   2010   Year
           
  X = single-core era    O = multi-core era begins
```

| Year | Processor | Clock Speed | TDP (Watts) |
|------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| 1993 | Pentium | 66 MHz | 15 W |
| 1999 | Pentium III | 500 MHz | 25 W |
| 2004 | Pentium 4 | 3.8 GHz | 115 W |
| 2006 | Core 2 Duo | 2.4 GHz | 65 W |
| 2023 | Core i9-14900K | 6.0 GHz | 253 W |

Notice what happened around 2006: Intel **lowered** clock speeds and added a second core instead. The free lunch was over.

### The Multi-Core Era

Since we cannot make individual cores much faster, chip designers pivoted to putting **more cores** on a single die. Modern CPUs have 8-24 cores. But here is the problem: most software is written **sequentially**. A program that runs on one core cannot magically use 8 cores. Developers must explicitly restructure their code for parallelism.

This is where the story gets interesting. CPUs added cores cautiously -- 2, 4, 8, 16. GPUs took a radically different approach: they went to **thousands** of simpler cores.

sections.title: Throughput vs Latency
sections.content:
To understand why GPUs exist, you need to understand two fundamentally different computing philosophies: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CPU["CPU Core - Latency-optimized"] direction TB CC["Control: Branch Predictor, OoO Engine,
Speculative Exec, Register Renaming"] CA["ALU | ALU | ALU | ALU"] CL1["L1 48KB"] CL2["L2 1.25MB"] CL3["L3 32MB shared"] CC --> CA --> CL1 --> CL2 --> CL3 end subgraph GPU["GPU SM - Throughput-optimized"] direction TB GA["48+ ALUs (8 rows x 6 cols)"] GS["Tiny cache + Shared Memory"] GA --> GS end CPU -. "~80% control / ~20% ALU
One thread fast" .-> GPU GPU -. "~80% ALU / ~20% control
Thousands of threads" .-> CPU classDef control fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fb923c,color:#fed7aa classDef alu fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#bbf7d0 class CC,CL1,CL2,CL3 control class CA,GA,GS alu ``` **Latency-oriented design (CPUs):** Minimize the time to complete **one** task. CPUs have massive caches (often 32+ MB of L3 cache), sophisticated branch predictors, out-of-order execution engines, and speculative execution. All of this hardware exists to make a single thread of execution as fast as possible. > **Analogy:** A CPU is like a Ferrari. It gets **one person** to their destination incredibly fast. It has a powerful engine, excellent handling, and advanced technology. But it can only carry one or two people at a time. **Throughput-oriented design (GPUs):** Maximize the total work completed per unit time across **all** tasks, even if each individual task is slower. GPUs have tiny caches, no branch prediction, simple in-order cores -- but thousands of them. > **Analogy:** A GPU is like a city bus system. Each individual bus is slower than a Ferrari. But 100 buses running simultaneously move 5,000 people per hour, while 100 Ferraris move only 200. The key insight is: **many real-world problems are embarrassingly parallel.** Processing 10 million pixels in an image, multiplying two 4096x4096 matrices, computing gradients for a neural network with 175 billion parameters -- these problems decompose naturally into millions of independent sub-tasks. > **Quick check:** You need to add two arrays of 10 million numbers. A CPU processes them one-at-a-time at 1 billion ops/sec. A GPU has 2,560 slower cores, each doing 500 million ops/sec. Which finishes first, and by how much? (Open-ended -- work it out: 10M / 1e9 = 10ms on CPU; 10M / (2560 * 5e8) ~ 0.0078ms of pure compute on GPU. The GPU advantage is enormous on compute, but transfer overhead can change the picture, as we will see in the heterogeneous-computing section.) ### Performance Numbers That Matter To appreciate the scale difference, consider these peak theoretical throughputs: | Metric | Intel i9-14900K (CPU) | NVIDIA A100 (GPU) | Ratio | |--------|----------------------|-------------------|-------| | FP32 TFLOPS | ~1.0 | 19.5 | 20x | | FP16 TFLOPS | ~2.0 | 312 | 156x | | Memory BW (GB/s) | 89.6 | 2,039 | 23x | | Cores | 24 | 6,912 | 288x | | TDP (Watts) | 253 | 300 | 1.2x | The GPU achieves **156x more half-precision FLOPS** while consuming only 20% more power. This is not magic -- it is a fundamentally different design tradeoff. The GPU sacrifices single-thread performance to maximize aggregate throughput. > **Footnote on FP16:** The A100 312 TFLOPS figure includes Tensor Core acceleration; the CPU ~2 TFLOPS uses AVX-512 FMA. The comparison is somewhat apples-to-pears, but it captures the practical gap that deep-learning workloads see. For the **T4 GPU** you will likely use on Google Colab: - 2,560 CUDA cores, 8.1 TFLOPS FP32 (counting fused multiply-add as 2 ops, the standard marketing convention) - 16 GB GDDR6 at 320 GB/s bandwidth - 70W TDP (very power-efficient) - On T4: vector addition of 10M floats takes roughly 0.3 ms of pure GPU compute; with PCIe transfers included the round-trip is closer to 3-4 ms, still faster than a pure-Python loop but barely faster than NumPy on a strong CPU. Arithmetic intensity matters -- we will quantify this in the heterogeneous-computing sections.

sections.title: Why It Matters & Common Errors
sections.content:
If you are training deep learning models, doing scientific simulations, processing large datasets, or working on computer graphics, the GPU is not just "faster" -- it is a **different category** of computing device. Learning to program GPUs is not about learning a new language; it is about learning to think in parallel.

The rest of this course will teach you that parallel thinking, using CUDA through Python's `numba.cuda` library. By the end, you will be writing kernels that run thousands of threads simultaneously, achieving speedups of 10-100x over CPU code.

```python
# A taste of what is coming: CPU vs GPU vector addition
import numpy as np
import time

# CPU: sequential processing
def cpu_vector_add(a, b, c):
    for i in range(len(a)):
        c[i] = a[i] + b[i]

n = 10_000_000
a = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
b = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
c = np.zeros(n, dtype=np.float32)

start = time.perf_counter()
cpu_vector_add(a, b, c)
cpu_time = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"CPU (pure Python loop): {cpu_time*1000:.1f} ms")

# NumPy (vectorized, uses SIMD on CPU)
start = time.perf_counter()
c = a + b
np_time = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"NumPy (vectorized CPU): {np_time*1000:.1f} ms")
print(f"Speedup from vectorization: {cpu_time/np_time:.0f}x")
print("Imagine another 10-100x on top of that -- that is GPU computing.")
```

### What CUDA Actually Is

A quick mental model for the absolute beginner: **CUDA** is NVIDIA's stack for general-purpose GPU computing. It has three layers you should be aware of:

- **Driver:** the kernel-level NVIDIA driver that talks to the GPU. Installed once on your system (or pre-installed on Colab).
- **Runtime / toolkit:** libraries (`libcudart`, `cuBLAS`, `cuDNN`) and the compiler `nvcc` that turns CUDA C++ into **PTX** (a virtual ISA) and then into GPU machine code.
- **Bindings:** higher-level wrappers like `numba.cuda` (Python, what we use), PyCUDA, CuPy, and PyTorch/JAX. They generate PTX under the hood and call the runtime for you.

When you `!pip install numba` on Colab, the toolkit is already there. On your own machine, "installing CUDA" means installing the driver plus the toolkit version your library expects. We will not write `nvcc` invocations directly -- numba handles compilation transparently.

### Common Error Messages When Getting Started

Before we dive deeper, here are errors you may hit when first setting up CUDA. Recognizing them early will save you hours:

```
numba.cuda.cudadrv.error.CudaSupportError: Error at driver init:
  CUDA driver library cannot be found.
```
This means no NVIDIA GPU driver is installed. On Colab, make sure you selected **Runtime > Change runtime type > T4 GPU**.

```
numba.cuda.cudadrv.driver.CudaAPIError: [700] Call to cuMemAlloc results in
  CUDA_ERROR_ILLEGAL_ADDRESS
```
This usually means a previous kernel crashed and corrupted the GPU state. Restart your runtime (Runtime > Restart runtime).

> **Gotcha:** If you see `CudaSupportError` on Colab, the most common cause is forgetting to enable the GPU runtime. Go to Runtime > Change runtime type and select T4 GPU. This catches about 90% of students in their first session.

This module will take you from understanding **why** GPUs exist to writing your first GPU kernel. Let us start by looking at what makes GPU architecture so different.
sections.code:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time

# ============================================
# Demonstrating why parallel computing matters
# ============================================

# Task: Add two large vectors element-by-element
# This is "embarrassingly parallel" -- each element is independent

n = 10_000_000
a = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
b = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)

# Method 1: Pure Python loop (sequential, one element at a time)
def python_add(a, b):
    c = np.empty_like(a)
    for i in range(len(a)):
        c[i] = a[i] + b[i]
    return c

# Time the pure Python approach (only first 100K to avoid waiting)
small_a, small_b = a[:100_000], b[:100_000]
start = time.perf_counter()
c_py = python_add(small_a, small_b)
python_time = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"Pure Python (100K elements): {python_time*1000:.1f} ms")
print(f"  Estimated for 10M elements: {python_time*100*1000:.0f} ms")

# Method 2: NumPy vectorized (uses SIMD instructions on CPU)
start = time.perf_counter()
c_np = a + b
numpy_time = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"\nNumPy vectorized (10M elements): {numpy_time*1000:.2f} ms")
print(f"  Speedup vs Python: {(python_time*100)/numpy_time:.0f}x")

# Method 3: GPU with numba.cuda
from numba import cuda

@cuda.jit
def gpu_add(a, b, c):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)  # Get global thread index
    if idx < a.size:     # Boundary check
        c[idx] = a[idx] + b[idx]

# Transfer data to GPU
d_a = cuda.to_device(a)
d_b = cuda.to_device(b)
d_c = cuda.device_array_like(a)

# Configure grid: 256 threads per block
threads_per_block = 256
blocks_per_grid = (n + threads_per_block - 1) // threads_per_block

# Warm up
gpu_add[blocks_per_grid, threads_per_block](d_a, d_b, d_c)
cuda.synchronize()

# Timed run
start = time.perf_counter()
gpu_add[blocks_per_grid, threads_per_block](d_a, d_b, d_c)
cuda.synchronize()
gpu_time = time.perf_counter() - start

c_gpu = d_c.copy_to_host()
print(f"\nGPU numba.cuda (10M elements): {gpu_time*1000:.2f} ms")
print(f"  Speedup vs NumPy: {numpy_time/gpu_time:.1f}x")
print(f"  Speedup vs Python: {(python_time*100)/gpu_time:.0f}x")

# Verify correctness
assert np.allclose(c_np, c_gpu, atol=1e-5), "Results don't match!"
print("\nResults verified: GPU output matches CPU output.")

references.title: PMPP Chapter 1: Introduction (Hwu, Kirk, El Hajj)
The Free Lunch Is Over (Herb Sutter, 2005)

sections.title: Two Different Machines
sections.content:
A CPU and a GPU are both silicon chips that execute instructions. But they are designed for completely different workloads, and understanding their architectural differences is essential to writing efficient GPU code.

### CPU Architecture: The Swiss Army Knife

A modern CPU core is an engineering marvel optimized for **sequential speed**. Let us break down where the transistors go:

**Control Logic (~50% of die area):** The CPU dedicates enormous resources to making each individual instruction execute as fast as possible:
- **Branch Predictor:** Guesses which way an `if/else` will go before the condition is evaluated. Modern CPUs achieve 95%+ prediction accuracy. When wrong, the pipeline must flush (15-20 cycle penalty).
- **Out-of-Order Execution Engine:** Reorders instructions on-the-fly to avoid stalls. If instruction B depends on A's result but instruction C does not, the CPU runs C while waiting for A.
- **Speculative Execution:** Executes instructions ahead of time, discarding results if the speculation was wrong.
- **Register Renaming:** Maps logical registers to a larger set of physical registers to eliminate false data dependencies.

**Cache Hierarchy (~30% of die area):** CPUs have large, multi-level caches to hide memory latency:

| Cache Level | Size (typical) | Latency | Purpose |
|-------------|---------------|---------|----------|
| L1 Data | 48 KB/core | ~4 cycles | Hot loop variables |
| L1 Instruction | 32 KB/core | ~4 cycles | Current code |
| L2 | 1.25 MB/core | ~12 cycles | Working set |
| L3 (shared) | 32-36 MB | ~40 cycles | Shared data |
| Main Memory | 16-128 GB | ~200 cycles | Everything else |

**ALUs (~20% of die area):** The actual computation units are a surprisingly small fraction of the chip. A modern CPU core might have 4-8 ALUs (Arithmetic Logic Units).

> **Key insight:** Most of the CPU's transistors are spent on making **one thread** run fast, not on doing more computations in parallel.

### GPU Architecture: The Massively Parallel Engine

A GPU flips the CPU's priorities upside down. Instead of making one thread fast, it makes **thousands of threads efficient**.

**ALUs (~80% of die area):** The vast majority of the GPU die is dedicated to computation units. An NVIDIA A100 has 6,912 CUDA cores (FP32 ALUs) and 432 Tensor Cores.

**Minimal Control Logic (~5%):** GPU cores have no branch prediction, no out-of-order execution, no speculative execution. Each core is simple: fetch instruction, decode, execute. Period.

**Small Caches (~15%):** GPUs have tiny caches per core. An A100 has only 192 KB of L1/shared memory per Streaming Multiprocessor (SM), compared to ~1.3 MB of L1+L2 per CPU core.

### Memory Hierarchy at a Glance

The GPU exposes a much more explicit memory hierarchy than CPUs do, and reasoning about it is the single biggest performance lever in CUDA. Here is the pyramid you will return to in Module 3:

| Level | Scope | Approx. latency | Approx. bandwidth | Size per SM (T4) |
|-------|-------|-----------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Registers | per-thread | ~0 cycles | (free) | 256 KB (65,536 x 4B) |
| Shared memory / L1 | per-block | ~5-30 cycles | ~10 TB/s aggregate | 64 KB |
| L2 cache | device-wide | ~150-200 cycles | ~2 TB/s | 4 MB total |
| Global (DRAM) | device-wide | ~400-800 cycles | 320 GB/s (T4) | 16 GB total |

The key takeaway for now: a register hit is essentially free, while a global-memory miss costs hundreds of cycles. **Module 3** will show you how to keep hot data in registers and shared memory, and we will revisit arithmetic intensity in that context.

sections.title: Latency Hiding & SIMT
sections.content:
If GPU cores are so simple and have tiny caches, how do they achieve such high throughput? The answer is **massive thread-level parallelism** as a latency-hiding mechanism.

When a CPU thread hits a cache miss (waiting ~200 cycles for memory), the core stalls -- it has nothing else to do. The out-of-order engine tries to find other instructions to run, but there are limits.

When a GPU thread hits a memory access, the hardware instantly **switches to another thread** at zero cost. There is no context switch overhead because the GPU keeps the register state of thousands of threads resident simultaneously. While threads 0-31 wait for memory, threads 32-63 execute. When those stall, threads 64-95 take over. And so on.

```mermaid
gantt
  title Warp Scheduling Timeline (GPU never idle)
  dateFormat X
  axisFormat %s
  section Warp 0
  Compute      :done,    w0a, 0, 50
  Mem stall    :crit,    w0b, 50, 350
  Compute      :done,    w0c, 350, 400
  section Warp 1
  Waiting      :         w1a, 0, 50
  Compute      :done,    w1b, 50, 100
  Mem stall    :crit,    w1c, 100, 350
  Compute      :done,    w1d, 350, 400
  section Warp 2
  Waiting      :         w2a, 0, 100
  Compute      :done,    w2b, 100, 150
  Mem stall    :crit,    w2c, 150, 350
  Compute      :done,    w2d, 350, 400
  section Warp 3
  Waiting      :         w3a, 0, 150
  Compute      :done,    w3b, 150, 200
  Mem stall    :crit,    w3c, 200, 400
  section SM busy
  100% utilized:active,   sm,  0, 400
```

> **Analogy:** Imagine a restaurant kitchen. A CPU chef starts a dish, and when it needs to simmer for 10 minutes, the chef stands there watching the pot. A GPU chef starts 100 dishes simultaneously -- when dish 1 simmers, the chef works on dish 2, then dish 3. The chef is never idle because there is always another dish ready for attention.

This is why GPUs need **thousands of active threads** to achieve peak performance. If you launch only 32 threads on a GPU, it will be slower than a CPU because those 32 simple cores are each slower than a CPU core, and there are not enough threads to hide memory latency.

> **Quick check:** A GPU SM can hold 2,048 threads. Memory latency is ~400 cycles. Each thread computes for ~20 cycles between memory accesses. How many warps (of 32 threads) does the SM need to keep busy during a stall? (Answer: 400/20 = 20 warps = 640 threads minimum. That is why high **occupancy** -- the ratio of active warps to the SM maximum, defined formally in Module 5 -- matters.)

### SIMT: Single Instruction, Multiple Threads

GPU threads do not execute independently like CPU threads. They execute in groups of 32 called **warps** (NVIDIA terminology). All 32 threads in a warp execute the **same instruction** at the **same time**, but on different data.

This is similar to SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) on CPUs, but with an important difference: SIMT threads have their own program counter and can **diverge**. If a warp hits an `if/else`:

```python
if condition:    # Some threads: True, others: False
    do_A()       # Only threads where condition=True execute
else:
    do_B()       # Only threads where condition=False execute
# All threads reconverge here
```

```
  Warp Divergence: What Actually Happens
  
  32 threads in a warp hit an if/else:
  
  Thread:  0  1  2  3  4  5  6  7  ...  30 31
  Cond:    T  F  T  T  F  F  T  F  ...  T  F
  
  Pass 1 (if-branch):   Active threads marked with #
  Thread:  #  .  #  #  .  .  #  .  ...  #  .
           ^     ^  ^        ^        ^       = 16 threads active
           Executing do_A()...  (other 16 IDLE but still consuming a slot)
  
  Pass 2 (else-branch): Active threads marked with #
  Thread:  .  #  .  .  #  #  .  #  ...  .  #
              ^        ^  ^     ^           ^ = 16 threads active
              Executing do_B()...  (other 16 IDLE)
  
  Result: 2 passes instead of 1 = 50% throughput!
  Worst case: only 1 thread diverges = still 2 full passes = ~50% waste
```

Both branches execute **sequentially**: first the `if` branch for qualifying threads (others are masked/idle), then the `else` branch. This is called **warp divergence** and it can sharply reduce your throughput.

> **Gotcha:** Warp divergence penalties are per-warp, not per-thread. Even if only 1 thread out of 32 takes the other branch, the ENTIRE warp must execute both paths. The slowdown depends on how much work is in each branch -- if the branches are roughly equal in length, you halve throughput; if one branch is short, the cost is smaller.

> **Rule of thumb:** Avoid `if/else` statements where different threads in the same warp take different paths. When divergence is unavoidable, minimize the work in the divergent branches.

sections.title: Streaming Multiprocessors
sections.content:
GPU cores are organized into groups called **Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs)**. Each SM contains: - 64-128 CUDA cores (depending on architecture) - Shared memory (configurable, typically 48-164 KB) - L1 cache - Warp schedulers (2-4 per SM) - Register file (65,536 32-bit registers on many architectures) The NVIDIA T4 GPU (common on Google Colab) has: - **40 SMs** - **64 FP32 cores per SM** = 2,560 total CUDA cores - **320 Tensor Cores** (for matrix operations) - **16 GB GDDR6 memory** at 320 GB/s bandwidth - **65,536 registers per SM** ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph T4["NVIDIA T4 GPU - 40 SMs - 2,560 CUDA cores - 8.1 TFLOPS FP32"] direction TB subgraph row0["Row 0"] direction LR S0[SM 0] ~~~ S1[SM 1] ~~~ S2[SM 2] ~~~ S3[SM 3] ~~~ S4[SM 4] ~~~ S5[SM 5] ~~~ S6[SM 6] ~~~ S7[SM 7] ~~~ S8[SM 8] ~~~ S9[SM 9] end subgraph row1["Row 1"] direction LR S10[SM 10] ~~~ S11[SM 11] ~~~ S12[SM 12] ~~~ S13[SM 13] ~~~ S14[SM 14] ~~~ S15[SM 15] ~~~ S16[SM 16] ~~~ S17[SM 17] ~~~ S18[SM 18] ~~~ S19[SM 19] end subgraph row2["Row 2"] direction LR S20[SM 20] ~~~ S21[SM 21] ~~~ S22[SM 22] ~~~ S23[SM 23] ~~~ S24[SM 24] ~~~ S25[SM 25] ~~~ S26[SM 26] ~~~ S27[SM 27] ~~~ S28[SM 28] ~~~ S29[SM 29] end subgraph row3["Row 3"] direction LR S30[SM 30] ~~~ S31[SM 31] ~~~ S32[SM 32] ~~~ S33[SM 33] ~~~ S34[SM 34] ~~~ S35[SM 35] ~~~ S36[SM 36] ~~~ S37[SM 37] ~~~ S38[SM 38] ~~~ S39[SM 39] end detail["Per SM: 64 cores, 64KB shmem, 65,536 regs, 4 warp schedulers
Memory: 16 GB GDDR6 @ 320 GB/s"] end classDef sm fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#60a5fa,color:#dbeafe class S0,S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S9,S10,S11,S12,S13,S14,S15,S16,S17,S18,S19,S20,S21,S22,S23,S24,S25,S26,S27,S28,S29,S30,S31,S32,S33,S34,S35,S36,S37,S38,S39 sm ``` Threads are assigned to SMs in units called **thread blocks** (or simply "blocks"). All threads in a block run on the same SM and can share data through shared memory. Threads in different blocks cannot directly communicate (except through global memory). ### A Quick Note on Compute Capability NVIDIA labels each GPU generation with a **compute capability** (CC) number that determines which instructions and features your hardware supports: | GPU | Architecture | Compute Capability | Notable features | |-----|--------------|--------------------|--------------------| | T4 | Turing | 7.5 | Tensor Cores (FP16/INT8) | | V100 | Volta | 7.0 | First Tensor Cores | | A100 | Ampere | 8.0 | Async copy, BF16 | | H100 | Hopper | 9.0 | TMA, FP8, thread-block clusters | Query yours with `cuda.get_current_device().compute_capability`. Some features (like asynchronous shared-memory copy) require CC >= 8.0; numba and CUDA libraries will silently fall back where possible. We will mostly target CC 7.5 (T4) so everything in this course runs on Colab's free tier. ### Think Like a GPU: Your First Mental Model Here is the mental shift that separates beginners from effective GPU programmers. When you see a problem like "multiply every element in an array by 2", your CPU brain thinks: **CPU thinking:** "I will loop through the array, element by element, multiplying each one." **GPU thinking:** "I AM one of 10 million threads. I only care about MY element. I multiply it by 2. I do not know or care what other threads are doing. There is no loop." This is profound. In GPU programming, you write code from the perspective of a SINGLE thread. The parallelism comes from launching millions of copies of that single-thread code. You never write a for-loop over your data -- instead, each thread figures out WHICH piece of data it owns (via its thread index) and processes just that piece. In Module 2, you will put this mental model into practice by writing your first kernel and learning exactly how threads identify which data to work on.

sections.title: What This Means for You
sections.content:
### What Would Happen If...

- **What if you launched only 32 threads on a T4 GPU?** You would use 1 warp on 1 SM. The other 39 SMs sit completely idle. Even that 1 SM cannot hide memory latency with just 1 warp. Performance would likely be WORSE than a CPU.

- **What if every thread in your kernel had a different if/else path?** Warp divergence would serialize execution. In the extreme case (32 unique code paths per warp), you get 1/32 throughput -- essentially sequential execution on very slow cores.

- **What if the GPU had no way to switch between warps?** Every memory access (400+ cycles) would stall the entire SM. The GPU would achieve less than 1% of its peak FLOPS. Thread-level parallelism is not a nice-to-have; it is the fundamental mechanism that makes GPUs work.

### What This Means for You as a Programmer

1. **Launch enough threads.** A GPU with 2,560 cores needs tens of thousands of threads to stay busy. Launching 100 threads wastes 96% of the hardware.
2. **Minimize branching.** Warp divergence serializes execution. Structure your code so adjacent threads follow the same path.
3. **Think about data locality.** Memory accesses are expensive. In Module 3, you will learn about the GPU memory hierarchy and how shared memory can deliver 100x faster access than global memory.
4. **Prefer simple, uniform computation.** GPU cores are simple. Complex control flow with many branches destroys performance.

### Does This Knowledge Transfer to Non-NVIDIA GPUs?

Mostly yes. The SIMT mental model -- threads, warps (called "wavefronts" of 32 or 64 on AMD), shared memory, occupancy -- is universal across modern GPUs. AMD's HIP is nearly source-compatible with CUDA, Apple's Metal compute shaders use the same parallel-decomposition mindset, and SYCL/OpenCL/Vulkan compute all share the same fundamentals. The CUDA-specific syntax you will learn translates directly.
sections.code:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda

# ============================================
# Exploring GPU hardware properties
# ============================================

# Query GPU device information
device = cuda.get_current_device()
print("=== GPU Device Information ===")
print(f"Device name: {device.name}")
print(f"Compute capability: {device.compute_capability}")
print(f"Max threads per block: {device.MAX_THREADS_PER_BLOCK}")
print(f"Max block dimensions: {device.MAX_BLOCK_DIM_X} x {device.MAX_BLOCK_DIM_Y} x {device.MAX_BLOCK_DIM_Z}")
print(f"Max grid dimensions: {device.MAX_GRID_DIM_X} x {device.MAX_GRID_DIM_Y} x {device.MAX_GRID_DIM_Z}")
print(f"Warp size: {device.WARP_SIZE}")
print(f"Max shared memory per block: {device.MAX_SHARED_MEMORY_PER_BLOCK} bytes")
print(f"Multiprocessor count: {device.MULTIPROCESSOR_COUNT}")

# ============================================
# Demonstrating SIMT and warp divergence
# ============================================

# Kernel WITHOUT divergence: all threads take the same path
@cuda.jit
def no_divergence_kernel(data, out):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < data.size:
        # All threads do the same work
        out[idx] = data[idx] * 2.0 + 1.0

# Kernel WITH divergence: odd/even threads take different paths
@cuda.jit
def divergent_kernel(data, out):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < data.size:
        if idx % 2 == 0:  # Half the warp goes one way
            out[idx] = data[idx] * 2.0 + 1.0
        else:              # Other half goes the other way
            out[idx] = data[idx] * 3.0 - 1.0

n = 10_000_000
data = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
d_data = cuda.to_device(data)
d_out = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)

threads = 256
blocks = (n + threads - 1) // threads

# Warm up
no_divergence_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
cuda.synchronize()
divergent_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
cuda.synchronize()

# Benchmark: No divergence
iterations = 100
start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iterations):
    no_divergence_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
cuda.synchronize()
no_div_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iterations

# Benchmark: With divergence
start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(iterations):
    divergent_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, d_out)
cuda.synchronize()
div_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / iterations

print(f"\n=== Warp Divergence Impact ===")
print(f"No divergence: {no_div_time*1000:.3f} ms")
print(f"With divergence: {div_time*1000:.3f} ms")
print(f"Slowdown from divergence: {div_time/no_div_time:.2f}x")
print("\nNote: Even simple odd/even branching causes measurable slowdown.")
print("In real kernels with complex branching, the penalty can be much worse.")

references.title: PMPP Chapter 2: Heterogeneous Data Parallel Computing
CUDA C++ Programming Guide - SIMT Architecture

sections.title: Host/Device & PCIe
sections.content:
GPU computing is fundamentally **heterogeneous**: your program runs on two different processors simultaneously. The terminology is precise and consistent throughout CUDA programming: - **Host:** The CPU and its memory (system RAM). Your Python code runs here. Control flow, file I/O, network requests, and orchestration happen on the host. - **Device:** The GPU and its memory (VRAM/GDDR). Massively parallel computation happens here. The GPU cannot access your filesystem, network, or most OS services. A typical GPU program follows this pattern: ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CPU as CPU (Host) participant PCIe as PCIe Bus (~32 GB/s) participant GPU as GPU (Device) Note over CPU: 1. Prepare data
(load, preprocess) CPU->>PCIe: 2. cuda.to_device(data) PCIe->>GPU: transfer Note over GPU: 3. Kernel executes
(thousands of threads) GPU->>PCIe: 4. .copy_to_host() PCIe->>CPU: transfer Note over CPU: 5. Use results
(save, display) ``` This is not just a software abstraction -- it reflects the physical hardware. The CPU and GPU are separate chips connected by a bus: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph host["CPU side (Host)"] CPU["CPU"] RAM["System RAM
16-512 GB
~50 GB/s"] CPU --- RAM end subgraph device["GPU side (Device)"] GPU["GPU"] VRAM["GPU VRAM
8-80 GB
~2000 GB/s"] GPU --- VRAM end host <-->|"PCIe 4: ~32 GB/s
PCIe 5: ~64 GB/s
(~60x bottleneck vs VRAM)"| device classDef slow fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#fecaca classDef fast fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#bbf7d0 class RAM slow class VRAM fast ``` ### The PCIe Bottleneck Notice the bandwidth numbers. GPU VRAM bandwidth is ~2000 GB/s (A100), but the PCIe bus connecting CPU and GPU is only ~32 GB/s (PCIe 4.0 x16). That is a **60x bottleneck**. > **Footnote on T4:** The T4 you will use on Colab is connected via **PCIe 3.0 x16**, which provides only ~16 GB/s. So your transfer ceiling is half of the PCIe 4 number quoted above. Whatever generation your GPU uses, the principle is identical: PCIe is dramatically slower than VRAM, and the gap dominates short kernels. This means that if you constantly transfer data back and forth between CPU and GPU, the transfer time will dominate your computation time. The PCIe bus becomes the straw through which you are trying to drink a firehose. > **Analogy:** Imagine a factory (GPU) that can process 2,000 packages per second, but the delivery truck (PCIe) can only bring in 32 packages per second. The factory workers are idle 98% of the time waiting for deliveries. The solution? Send large batches infrequently rather than small batches constantly.

sections.title: When to Use a GPU
sections.content:
GPUs are not universally faster than CPUs. They excel at specific types of problems:

**Good GPU workloads (high parallelism, regular computation):**
- Matrix multiplication and linear algebra
- Image/video processing (each pixel processed independently)
- Neural network training and inference
- Monte Carlo simulations
- Molecular dynamics
- Signal processing (FFTs)

**Poor GPU workloads (sequential, irregular, or small):**
- File I/O and string processing
- Complex branching logic (compilers, interpreters)
- Small problems (overhead exceeds computation)
- Recursive algorithms with unpredictable depth (note: many "tree" workloads such as BVH traversal in graphics or beam search can in fact run efficiently on GPUs once reformulated as iterative, breadth-first kernels -- we revisit this in Module 11)
- Problems with heavy inter-thread communication

The rule of thumb: **if you can describe the problem as "do the same thing to millions of items independently," a GPU will help.** If the problem is "make a sequence of decisions where each depends on the last," stick with the CPU.

### Amdahl's Law: The Limits of Parallelism

In 1967, Gene Amdahl formulated a law that governs every parallel computing effort. It states that the maximum speedup from parallelizing a program is limited by the portion that must remain sequential:

```
Speedup = 1 / (S + P/N)

where:
  S = fraction of the program that is sequential (cannot be parallelized)
  P = fraction that is parallelizable (P = 1 - S)
  N = number of parallel processors
```

Let us work through concrete examples:

**Example 1: 90% parallelizable (S=0.1, P=0.9)**

| N (processors) | Speedup | Efficiency |
|----------------|---------|------------|
| 1 | 1.0x | 100% |
| 10 | 5.3x | 53% |
| 100 | 9.2x | 9.2% |
| 1,000 | 9.9x | 0.99% |
| 10,000 | 9.99x | 0.1% |
| Infinity | **10x** | ~0% |

Even with infinite processors, 10% sequential code limits you to **10x speedup**. Adding more cores beyond a few hundred is completely wasted.

**Example 2: 99% parallelizable (S=0.01, P=0.99)**

| N (processors) | Speedup |
|----------------|---------|
| 100 | 50x |
| 1,000 | 91x |
| 10,000 | 99x |
| Infinity | **100x** |

With 99% parallelizable code, you can achieve up to 100x speedup. This is much better but still bounded.

**Example 3: Data transfer overhead**

Amdahl's Law applies to more than just computation. Consider a program where:
- Data preparation (sequential): 5% of total time
- Data transfer CPU->GPU: 10% of total time
- GPU computation (parallelizable): 75% of total time
- Data transfer GPU->CPU: 10% of total time

The sequential fraction is S = 0.05 + 0.10 + 0.10 = 0.25 (25%). Maximum speedup with infinite GPU cores: **4x**. The data transfer is killing your performance.

> **Note:** In practice, CUDA streams allow you to **overlap** data transfer with GPU computation, partially mitigating this bottleneck. We will introduce streams concretely in a later module; for now, just know that the sequential fractions in the table above can sometimes be hidden, not just minimized.

sections.title: Maximizing GPU Utilization
sections.content:
Given Amdahl's Law and the PCIe bottleneck, here are strategies that professional GPU programmers use: **1. Minimize transfers:** Keep data on the GPU as long as possible. If you have a pipeline of operations (load -> process -> transform -> output), do all processing on the GPU before transferring results back. ```python from numba import cuda import numpy as np # BAD: Transfer back and forth for each operation d_data = cuda.to_device(data) kernel_1[blocks, threads](d_data) # GPU intermediate = d_data.copy_to_host() # GPU -> CPU (slow!) d_data = cuda.to_device(intermediate) # CPU -> GPU (slow!) kernel_2[blocks, threads](d_data) # GPU result = d_data.copy_to_host() # GPU -> CPU # GOOD: Stay on GPU between kernels d_data = cuda.to_device(data) # CPU -> GPU (once) kernel_1[blocks, threads](d_data) # GPU kernel_2[blocks, threads](d_data) # GPU (data stays on device) result = d_data.copy_to_host() # GPU -> CPU (once) ``` > **Gotcha:** A very common beginner pattern is calling `.copy_to_host()` after every kernel to "check" intermediate results during debugging. Each round-trip transfer can cost 1-10ms for large arrays. If you have 10 kernels in a pipeline, unnecessary transfers can add 10-100ms to what should be a sub-millisecond computation. On T4: transferring 40MB (10M floats) takes ~1.5ms each way, while the actual computation might only be 0.3ms. **2. Use pinned (page-locked) host memory.** Default NumPy arrays live in *pageable* host memory: the OS may swap their pages to disk, so the CUDA driver must first stage the data through a hidden pinned buffer before sending it across PCIe. This roughly halves achievable bandwidth (typically ~6 GB/s pageable vs ~12 GB/s pinned on PCIe 3 x16). In numba, you allocate pinned memory like this: ```python from numba import cuda import numpy as np # Allocate a pinned (page-locked) host array pinned_a = cuda.pinned_array(n, dtype=np.float32) pinned_a[:] = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32) # fill it d_a = cuda.to_device(pinned_a) # transfers ~2x faster than from a regular ndarray ``` Use pinned memory for the host buffers you transfer most often. The trade-off: pinned memory cannot be paged out, so allocating gigabytes of it can starve the rest of your system. **3. Overlap computation with transfer:** Modern GPUs support concurrent data transfer and computation using CUDA streams. While the GPU processes batch N, you can transfer batch N+1. **4. Increase the parallel fraction:** The biggest gains come not from faster GPUs but from parallelizing more of your code. Converting even 5% more of your sequential code to parallel can have a dramatic effect at scale. **5. Use batching:** Instead of processing one image at a time (small problem, high overhead), process 64 images at once (large problem, amortized overhead). This is why deep learning uses mini-batches. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph va["Vector Add (low AI: 1 FLOP / 12 bytes)"] direction LR VA1["Transfer To
45%"] --- VA2["Compute
10%"] --- VA3["Transfer Back
45%"] end subgraph mm["Matrix Multiply (high AI: O(N) FLOP/byte)"] direction LR MM1["Xfer
2%"] --- MM2["Compute
96%"] --- MM3["Xfer
2%"] end classDef xfer fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#ef4444,color:#fecaca classDef cmp fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,color:#bbf7d0 class VA1,VA3,MM1,MM3 xfer class VA2,MM2 cmp ``` > **Quick check:** You have a pipeline with 3 GPU kernels. Each kernel takes 0.1ms. Transferring data to and from the GPU takes 2ms each way. What is the total time with the naive approach (transfer for each kernel) vs. the optimized approach (transfer once, run all kernels, transfer back)? What is the speedup? (Think before scrolling.) ### Kernel-Launch Overhead and the "Is My Problem Big Enough?" Rule Every kernel launch is an RPC to the GPU: arguments are marshaled, queued on a stream, and the driver sends a command to the device. On consumer hardware this fixed cost is typically **10-30 microseconds**, regardless of how much work the kernel actually does. This has a sharp practical consequence. If your CPU version of the work takes less than ~1 millisecond, the GPU often loses, because launch overhead alone consumes a meaningful fraction of that budget -- and you still pay for PCIe transfers on top. A small loop that calls a tiny GPU kernel thousands of times in Python is the worst-case pattern: launches dominate, the GPU spends most of its time idle, and the CPU was never the bottleneck. A pragmatic sizing chart: | CPU baseline time | Likely GPU outcome | |-------------------|--------------------| | < 100 us | GPU strictly worse (overhead-bound) | | 100 us - 1 ms | Roughly tied; not worth the complexity | | 1 - 10 ms | Modest 2-5x speedup if data already on device | | > 10 ms with high arithmetic intensity | 10-100x speedup is realistic | **The fix when work is too small:** batch many small problems into one large kernel launch (deep learning's mini-batch trick), or fuse adjacent kernels into a single one so the launch cost is amortized. ### The Cost-Benefit Analysis Before reaching for a GPU, ask yourself: 1. **Is the computation large enough?** Launching a kernel has overhead (~10-30 microseconds). If your computation takes less than 1 millisecond on CPU, the GPU overhead may exceed the speedup. 2. **Is the problem parallelizable?** Can you decompose it into thousands of independent sub-tasks? 3. **Can you minimize transfers?** If you must transfer data every iteration, the PCIe bottleneck may negate the GPU speedup. 4. **Is the arithmetic intensity high enough?** Arithmetic intensity = FLOPs / bytes transferred from memory. Low arithmetic intensity means you are memory-bound, and the GPU's compute advantage is wasted. We will explore this concept deeply in Module 3 when we study the GPU memory hierarchy. | Arithmetic Intensity | Classification | Example | GPU Benefit | |---------------------|----------------|---------|-------------| | < 1 FLOP/byte | Memory-bound | Vector addition | Moderate | | 1-10 FLOP/byte | Mixed | Convolution | Good | | > 10 FLOP/byte | Compute-bound | Matrix multiply | Excellent | Matrix multiplication has arithmetic intensity of O(N) -- as matrices grow, each byte of data is used for more and more computations. This is why GPUs dominate linear algebra and deep learning. ### What Would Happen If... - **What if you transferred 1 float at a time to the GPU?** PCIe has per-transaction overhead of ~1-5 microseconds. Transferring 10M floats one at a time: 10M * 5us = 50 seconds. Transferring as one batch: ~1.5ms. That is a 33,000x difference. Always batch your transfers. - **What if 50% of your program were sequential?** Amdahl's Law says maximum speedup = 1/0.5 = 2x, regardless of GPU power. If your CPU version takes 10 seconds, the best you can achieve is 5 seconds. You would spend money on a GPU for a 2x speedup that could have been achieved with simple CPU optimization. - **What if GPUs had unlimited PCIe bandwidth?** Transfer overhead would vanish, and even low-arithmetic-intensity operations like vector addition would see the full GPU speedup. This is actually the direction hardware is moving -- PCIe 5.0 doubles bandwidth, and technologies like NVLink provide 600+ GB/s. ### Looking Ahead In Module 2, you will learn the CUDA programming model -- how to write kernels, organize threads into blocks and grids, and calculate thread indices. The Host/Device model from this section becomes concrete: you will write code that runs on the host (Python), launches code on the device (CUDA kernels), and transfers data between them.
sections.code:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda

# ============================================
# Heterogeneous Computing: Host/Device Model
# ============================================

# Step 1: Prepare data on HOST (CPU)
print("=== Host/Device Data Transfer Demo ===")
n = 5_000_000
host_a = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
host_b = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
print(f"Created {n:,} element arrays on CPU")
print(f"Data size: {host_a.nbytes / 1e6:.1f} MB per array")

# Step 2: Transfer to DEVICE (GPU) -- measure the cost
start = time.perf_counter()
d_a = cuda.to_device(host_a)
d_b = cuda.to_device(host_b)
d_c = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)
cuda.synchronize()
transfer_to_time = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"\nCPU -> GPU transfer: {transfer_to_time*1000:.2f} ms")
print(f"  Effective bandwidth: {3 * host_a.nbytes / transfer_to_time / 1e9:.1f} GB/s")

# Step 3: Compute on DEVICE
@cuda.jit
def vector_add(a, b, c):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < a.size:
        c[idx] = a[idx] + b[idx]

threads = 256
blocks = (n + threads - 1) // threads

vector_add[blocks, threads](d_a, d_b, d_c)  # Warm up
cuda.synchronize()

start = time.perf_counter()
vector_add[blocks, threads](d_a, d_b, d_c)
cuda.synchronize()
compute_time = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"\nGPU compute: {compute_time*1000:.3f} ms")

# Step 4: Transfer results back to HOST
start = time.perf_counter()
host_c = d_c.copy_to_host()
transfer_from_time = time.perf_counter() - start
print(f"GPU -> CPU transfer: {transfer_from_time*1000:.2f} ms")

# Analysis: Where does the time go?
total = transfer_to_time + compute_time + transfer_from_time
print(f"\n=== Time Breakdown ===")
print(f"CPU -> GPU transfer: {transfer_to_time/total*100:.1f}%")
print(f"GPU computation:     {compute_time/total*100:.1f}%")
print(f"GPU -> CPU transfer: {transfer_from_time/total*100:.1f}%")
print(f"Total:               {total*1000:.2f} ms")

# ============================================
# Amdahl's Law Demonstration
# ============================================
print(f"\n=== Amdahl's Law ===")
print(f"Sequential fraction (transfers): {(transfer_to_time + transfer_from_time)/total*100:.1f}%")
print(f"Parallel fraction (compute):     {compute_time/total*100:.1f}%")
print(f"\nFor vector addition, transfer dominates because arithmetic intensity is LOW.")
print(f"(Only 1 FLOP per 12 bytes loaded -- 2 reads + 1 write of 4-byte floats)")

# ============================================
# Demonstrate: keeping data on GPU is key
# ============================================
print(f"\n=== Pipeline: Stay on GPU ===")

@cuda.jit
def scale_kernel(data, scalar):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < data.size:
        data[idx] *= scalar

# BAD: Transfer back after each step
start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(10):
    d_data = cuda.to_device(host_a)
    scale_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, 1.01)
    cuda.synchronize()
    host_a_tmp = d_data.copy_to_host()
cuda.synchronize()
bad_time = time.perf_counter() - start

# GOOD: Stay on GPU for all 10 steps
d_data = cuda.to_device(host_a)
start = time.perf_counter()
for _ in range(10):
    scale_kernel[blocks, threads](d_data, 1.01)
cuda.synchronize()
result = d_data.copy_to_host()
good_time = time.perf_counter() - start

print(f"BAD  (transfer each step):  {bad_time*1000:.2f} ms")
print(f"GOOD (stay on GPU):         {good_time*1000:.2f} ms")
print(f"Speedup from avoiding transfers: {bad_time/good_time:.1f}x")

references.title: CUDA C++ Programming Guide - Heterogeneous Programming

quiz.question: What is the 'power wall' in CPU design?
options: The maximum amount of RAM a CPU can address
The physical limit on how many transistors can fit on a chip
The cubic relationship between clock frequency and power consumption that prevents further clock speed increases
The thermal limit of silicon at 100 degrees Celsius
quiz.explanation: The power wall refers to the fact that power consumption grows roughly as Voltage^2 * Frequency. Since voltage must increase with frequency, power grows cubically with clock speed, making further frequency increases impractical due to heat dissipation limits.

quiz.question: Why do GPUs dedicate most of their transistor budget to ALUs rather than control logic?
options: GPUs don't need to execute instructions
GPUs hide memory latency by switching between thousands of threads instead of using branch prediction and out-of-order execution
ALUs are cheaper to manufacture than control logic
GPU programs never have conditional branches
quiz.explanation: GPUs use massive thread-level parallelism to hide latency: when one warp stalls on memory, the hardware instantly switches to another warp at zero cost. This eliminates the need for expensive branch predictors and out-of-order execution engines, freeing transistors for more compute units.

quiz.question: What is a 'warp' in NVIDIA GPU architecture?
options: A single GPU core
A group of 32 threads that execute the same instruction simultaneously
A data transfer unit between CPU and GPU
A unit of shared memory
quiz.explanation: A warp is a group of 32 threads that execute in SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads) fashion. All threads in a warp execute the same instruction at the same time, but each operates on different data. This is the fundamental unit of execution on NVIDIA GPUs.

quiz.question: What happens when threads in the same warp take different branches of an if/else statement?
options: The warp splits into two independent warps
Both branches execute sequentially, with non-participating threads masked off (warp divergence)
The GPU throws a runtime error
One branch is chosen randomly and the other is skipped
quiz.explanation: When threads in a warp diverge, both branches execute serially. First the 'if' branch runs (with non-qualifying threads masked), then the 'else' branch. When the branches are roughly equal in length this halves throughput; when they differ in length the cost is proportional to the total work across both branches. Threads reconverge after the branch.

quiz.question: According to Amdahl's Law, what is the maximum speedup of a program that is 95% parallelizable, even with infinite processors?
options: Unlimited
quiz.explanation: Amdahl's Law: Speedup = 1 / S, where S is the sequential fraction. With S = 0.05 (5% sequential), the maximum speedup is 1/0.05 = 20x. The 5% sequential portion becomes the bottleneck that no amount of parallelism can overcome.

quiz.question: Why is the PCIe bus a critical bottleneck in GPU computing?
options: PCIe is unreliable and loses data during transfer
PCIe bandwidth (~32 GB/s) is 60x slower than GPU memory bandwidth (~2000 GB/s), so frequent CPU-GPU transfers dominate total execution time
PCIe can only transfer integer data, not floating-point
PCIe requires the GPU to stop computing during transfers
quiz.explanation: GPU VRAM bandwidth is ~2000 GB/s but PCIe 4.0 x16 provides only ~32 GB/s -- a 60x gap. If your program frequently transfers data between CPU and GPU, the transfer time can dominate total execution time, negating the GPU's computational advantage.

quiz.question: Which workload type benefits LEAST from GPU acceleration?
options: Matrix multiplication of large matrices
Image filtering applied to every pixel independently
Recursive tree traversal with unpredictable branching
Training a neural network with mini-batches of 64 images
quiz.explanation: Recursive tree traversal involves sequential decisions (each step depends on the previous), irregular memory access patterns, and heavy branching -- all of which are poorly suited for GPUs. GPUs excel at regular, data-parallel workloads like matrix multiply, image processing, and neural network training.

quiz.question: What is 'arithmetic intensity' and why does it matter for GPU performance?
options: The number of GPU cores used; more cores means more intensity
The ratio of floating-point operations to bytes transferred from memory; higher intensity means the GPU can better utilize its compute capabilities
The clock frequency of the GPU; higher frequency means more intense computation
The complexity of the algorithm; more complex algorithms have higher intensity
quiz.explanation: Arithmetic intensity = FLOPs / bytes transferred. Operations with high arithmetic intensity (like matrix multiplication, O(N) FLOPs per byte) can keep the GPU's compute units busy because each byte of data is reused for many calculations. Low intensity operations (like vector addition, 1 FLOP per 12 bytes) are memory-bound and cannot fully utilize the GPU's compute power.

exercise.title: CPU vs GPU Vector Addition Benchmark
exercise.description:
Implement vector addition on both CPU and GPU, measure transfer times, compute times, and total times, then analyze the results using Amdahl's Law.

**Requirements:**
- Create random float32 vectors of sizes: 1000, 100000, 1000000, 10000000
- For each size, measure: (a) CPU computation time (using NumPy), (b) GPU transfer time (to + from), (c) GPU computation time, (d) total GPU time
- Compute the speedup as CPU_time / GPU_total_time for each size
- Also compute the 'ideal' speedup as CPU_time / GPU_compute_time (ignoring transfers)
- Print results in a clear table
- Discuss: at what size does the GPU become worthwhile, considering transfer overhead?

**Hints:**
- Use `cuda.to_device()` and `.copy_to_host()` for transfers
- Use `cuda.synchronize()` before timing measurements
- Warm up the GPU with one untimed kernel launch before benchmarking
- Use `time.perf_counter()` for precise timing
- The crossover point where GPU beats CPU depends on the operation's arithmetic intensity
exercise.starterCode:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda

# GPU kernel for vector addition
@cuda.jit
def gpu_vector_add(a, b, c):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < a.size:
        c[idx] = a[idx] + b[idx]

def benchmark_size(n, num_iters=5):
    """Benchmark CPU vs GPU vector addition for a given size."""
    a = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
    b = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)

    # CPU benchmark (NumPy)
    c_cpu = a + b  # Warm up
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(num_iters):
        c_cpu = a + b
    cpu_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / num_iters

    # GPU benchmark
    threads = 256
    blocks = (n + threads - 1) // threads

    # TODO: Measure GPU transfer time (host -> device)
    # TODO: Measure GPU compute time (kernel execution)
    # TODO: Measure GPU transfer back time (device -> host)
    # TODO: Calculate total GPU time
    # TODO: Verify correctness

    # Return: cpu_time, transfer_to, compute, transfer_from, total_gpu
    pass

# TODO: Test sizes [1_000, 100_000, 1_000_000, 10_000_000]
# TODO: Print formatted table with columns:
#   Size | CPU (ms) | GPU Total (ms) | GPU Compute (ms) | Transfer (ms) | Speedup | Ideal Speedup
# TODO: Identify the crossover point
exercise.solution:
!pip install numba

import numpy as np
import time
from numba import cuda

@cuda.jit
def gpu_vector_add(a, b, c):
    idx = cuda.grid(1)
    if idx < a.size:
        c[idx] = a[idx] + b[idx]

def benchmark_size(n, num_iters=5):
    """Benchmark CPU vs GPU vector addition for a given size."""
    a = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)
    b = np.random.randn(n).astype(np.float32)

    # CPU benchmark (NumPy)
    c_cpu = a + b  # Warm up
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(num_iters):
        c_cpu = a + b
    cpu_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / num_iters

    # GPU setup
    threads = 256
    blocks = (n + threads - 1) // threads

    # Warm up GPU
    d_a = cuda.to_device(a)
    d_b = cuda.to_device(b)
    d_c = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)
    gpu_vector_add[blocks, threads](d_a, d_b, d_c)
    cuda.synchronize()

    # Measure transfer to GPU (this includes the small device_array allocation,
    # which is mostly free; the dominant cost is the host->device copies of a and b).
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(num_iters):
        d_a = cuda.to_device(a)
        d_b = cuda.to_device(b)
        d_c = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)
        cuda.synchronize()
    transfer_to = (time.perf_counter() - start) / num_iters

    # Measure GPU compute
    d_a = cuda.to_device(a)
    d_b = cuda.to_device(b)
    d_c = cuda.device_array(n, dtype=np.float32)
    cuda.synchronize()
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(num_iters):
        gpu_vector_add[blocks, threads](d_a, d_b, d_c)
        cuda.synchronize()
    compute_time = (time.perf_counter() - start) / num_iters

    # Measure transfer from GPU
    start = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(num_iters):
        c_gpu = d_c.copy_to_host()
    transfer_from = (time.perf_counter() - start) / num_iters

    total_gpu = transfer_to + compute_time + transfer_from

    # Verify correctness
    assert np.allclose(c_cpu, c_gpu, atol=1e-5), "Results mismatch!"

    return cpu_time, transfer_to, compute_time, transfer_from, total_gpu

# Benchmark at different sizes
sizes = [1_000, 100_000, 1_000_000, 10_000_000]
print(f"{'Size':>12} | {'CPU (ms)':>10} | {'GPU Tot (ms)':>13} | {'Compute (ms)':>13} | {'Xfer (ms)':>10} | {'Speedup':>8} | {'Ideal':>8}")
print("-" * 95)

for n in sizes:
    cpu_t, xfer_to, compute, xfer_from, total = benchmark_size(n)
    xfer = xfer_to + xfer_from
    speedup = cpu_t / total if total > 0 else float('inf')
    ideal = cpu_t / compute if compute > 0 else float('inf')
    print(f"{n:>12,} | {cpu_t*1000:>10.3f} | {total*1000:>13.3f} | {compute*1000:>13.3f} | {xfer*1000:>10.3f} | {speedup:>7.2f}x | {ideal:>7.1f}x")

print("\nNote: GPU 'Ideal' ignores transfer overhead (data already on GPU).")
print("For small sizes, transfer overhead dominates and CPU wins.")
print("For large sizes, GPU compute speed wins despite transfer costs.")
print("Vector addition has LOW arithmetic intensity (1 FLOP / 12 bytes),")
print("so the crossover point is relatively large. Matrix multiplication")
print("(HIGH arithmetic intensity) would show GPU advantages at smaller sizes.")
